assessment.title: FAIR Principles Assessment Tool
assessment.description: Evaluate your dataset's compliance with FAIR principles through this comprehensive assessment

categories.title: Findable
categories.description: The first step in making data reusable is to ensure it can be found by both humans and computers.

questions.title: Unique and Persistent Identifiers
questions.question: Does your dataset have a unique and persistent identifier (like DOI, Handle, or UUID)?
questions.explanation: A unique identifier ensures your dataset can be consistently referenced and cited. Persistent identifiers like DOI remain valid even if the location changes.

options.label: Yes, we use DOI or similar persistent identifier
options.feedback: Excellent! DOI and similar persistent identifiers are the gold standard for dataset identification.

options.label: Yes, we use UUID or similar unique identifier
options.feedback: Good! UUIDs provide uniqueness but consider upgrading to persistent identifiers like DOI for better longevity.

options.label: We have some form of identifier but it's not globally unique
options.feedback: This is a start, but consider implementing globally unique identifiers for better discoverability.

options.label: No, we don't have unique identifiers
options.feedback: Consider implementing unique identifiers as a priority. Start with DOI or UUID systems.

tips: Consider Handle System for institutional repositories
Ensure identifiers are included in all metadata

questions.title: Rich Metadata
questions.question: How comprehensive is the metadata describing your dataset?
questions.explanation: Rich metadata helps both humans and machines understand what your dataset contains, how it was created, and how it can be used.

options.label: Comprehensive metadata including methodology, variables, and context
options.feedback: Excellent! Comprehensive metadata is crucial for dataset discovery and reuse.

options.label: Good metadata with basic information and some details
options.feedback: Good start! Consider adding more methodological details and context information.

options.label: Basic metadata with title, author, and description
options.feedback: Basic metadata is present but could be enriched with more details about methodology and context.

options.label: Minimal or no metadata
options.feedback: Metadata is essential for findability. Start with basic elements and gradually add more details.

tips: Include creation date, methodology, and data collection details

questions.title: Metadata-Data Relationship
questions.question: Does your metadata clearly identify which dataset it describes?
questions.explanation: Metadata should explicitly reference the dataset it describes, creating a clear connection between the two.

options.label: Yes, metadata explicitly references the dataset identifier
options.feedback: Perfect! Clear metadata-data relationships are essential for automated discovery.

options.label: The relationship is implicit but clear
options.feedback: Consider making the relationship more explicit by including dataset identifiers in metadata.

options.label: The relationship is unclear or ambiguous
options.feedback: Make the metadata-data relationship more explicit to improve automated discovery.

options.label: No clear relationship between metadata and data
options.feedback: Establish clear connections between metadata and data by including explicit references.

questions.title: Searchable Registry
questions.question: Are your data and metadata registered in a searchable resource?
questions.explanation: Registration in searchable catalogs, repositories, or databases makes your dataset discoverable by search engines and other researchers.

options.label: Registered in multiple searchable repositories/catalogs
options.feedback: Excellent! Multiple registrations increase discoverability across different communities.

options.label: Registered in institutional or disciplinary repository
options.feedback: Great! Institutional repositories provide good discoverability within your domain.

options.label: Registered in one searchable resource
options.feedback: Good start! Consider registering in additional relevant repositories for broader reach.

options.label: Not registered in any searchable resource
options.feedback: Consider registering in relevant repositories like Zenodo, institutional repositories, or disciplinary databases.

tips: Use institutional repositories for internal discoverability
Register in disciplinary databases for domain-specific discovery
Consider general repositories like Zenodo for broader reach

categories.title: Accessible
categories.description: Once users find your data, they need to know how to access it, possibly including authentication and authorization.

questions.title: Standard Communication Protocol
questions.question: Are your data and metadata accessible via standardized communication protocols?
questions.explanation: Standard protocols like HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, or APIs ensure that data can be accessed reliably by various systems and users.

options.label: Accessible via HTTPS and standardized APIs
options.feedback: Excellent! HTTPS and APIs provide secure and programmatic access.

options.label: Accessible via HTTPS/HTTP
options.feedback: Good! HTTP/HTTPS is widely supported. Consider adding API access for better integration.

options.label: Accessible via FTP or similar standard protocols
options.feedback: FTP works but consider upgrading to HTTPS for better security and compatibility.

options.label: Accessible only via proprietary or non-standard protocols
options.feedback: Consider implementing standard protocols for better accessibility and interoperability.

options.label: No standardized access method
options.feedback: Implement standard protocols like HTTPS for data access as a priority.

questions.title: Open Protocol
questions.question: Are the access protocols open, free, and universally implementable?
questions.explanation: Open protocols ensure that anyone can access your data without proprietary software or licensing restrictions.

options.label: Fully open and free protocols (HTTP/HTTPS, REST APIs)
options.feedback: Perfect! Open protocols ensure universal accessibility.

options.label: Mostly open with some proprietary elements
options.feedback: Good, but consider eliminating proprietary elements for better accessibility.

options.label: Mix of open and proprietary protocols
options.feedback: Work towards using only open protocols for maximum accessibility.

options.label: Primarily proprietary protocols
options.feedback: Consider migrating to open protocols to improve accessibility for all users.

questions.title: Authentication and Authorization
questions.question: Does your access protocol support authentication and authorization when needed?
questions.explanation: For sensitive data, proper authentication and authorization mechanisms protect data while still allowing legitimate access.

options.label: Robust authentication with fine-grained authorization
options.feedback: Excellent! Proper security controls protect data while enabling authorized access.

options.label: Basic authentication and authorization
options.feedback: Good foundation! Consider implementing more granular access controls if needed.

options.label: Minimal security controls
options.feedback: Consider strengthening authentication and authorization mechanisms.

options.label: Fully open access (no authentication needed)
options.feedback: Open access is great for public data! Ensure this matches your data sensitivity level.

options.label: No clear access mechanism
options.feedback: Implement clear access mechanisms with appropriate security controls.

questions.title: Persistent Metadata
questions.question: Will metadata remain accessible even if the data is no longer available?
questions.explanation: Persistent metadata ensures that information about your dataset remains available for citation and reference even after the data itself is removed.

options.label: Metadata persistence is guaranteed by repository/institution
options.feedback: Excellent! Persistent metadata ensures long-term discoverability and citation.

options.label: Metadata will likely persist but not guaranteed
options.feedback: Consider using repositories that guarantee long-term metadata preservation.

options.label: Uncertain about metadata persistence
options.feedback: Investigate metadata persistence policies and consider more reliable hosting options.

options.label: Metadata will be deleted with data
options.feedback: Implement metadata persistence to maintain long-term discoverability and citation capability.

categories.title: Interoperable
categories.description: Data usually needs to be integrated with other data, and work with applications or workflows for analysis, storage, and processing.

questions.title: Formal Language
questions.question: Do your data and metadata use a formal, accessible, shared, and broadly applicable language for knowledge representation?
questions.explanation: Using standard, machine-readable formats ensures that your data can be processed by various systems and integrated with other datasets.

options.label: JSON-LD, RDF, or other semantic web formats
options.feedback: Excellent! Semantic web formats enable rich machine-readable metadata and linking.

options.label: Structured formats like JSON, XML, or well-formed CSV
options.feedback: Good! These formats are machine-readable and widely supported.

options.label: Standard formats specific to the domain
options.feedback: Domain-specific standards are good but consider broader interoperability formats.

options.label: Proprietary or poorly structured formats
options.feedback: Consider migrating to more standard, interoperable formats.

options.label: No formal structure
options.feedback: Implement structured, machine-readable formats as a priority.

questions.title: FAIR Vocabularies
questions.question: Do your data and metadata use vocabularies that follow FAIR principles?
questions.explanation: Using controlled vocabularies and ontologies ensures consistent terminology and enables better data integration and discovery.

options.label: Multiple FAIR vocabularies and ontologies
options.feedback: Excellent! Using multiple FAIR vocabularies maximizes interoperability.

options.label: Domain-specific controlled vocabularies
options.feedback: Great! Domain vocabularies provide excellent semantic precision.

options.label: Basic controlled vocabularies
options.feedback: Good start! Consider adopting more comprehensive domain vocabularies.

options.label: Minimal use of controlled vocabularies
options.feedback: Increase use of controlled vocabularies for better semantic interoperability.

options.label: No controlled vocabularies
options.feedback: Implement controlled vocabularies starting with basic ones like ORCID for researchers.

tips: Adopt domain-specific vocabularies like MeSH for medical data

questions.title: Qualified References
questions.question: Do your data and metadata include qualified references to other metadata?
questions.explanation: Qualified references create explicit relationships between datasets, enabling better integration and understanding of data connections.

options.label: Comprehensive qualified references with relationship types
options.feedback: Perfect! Detailed relationship metadata enables rich data integration.

options.label: Basic references to related datasets
options.feedback: Good! Consider adding more specific relationship qualifiers.

options.label: Minimal or unqualified references
options.feedback: Add more qualified references to improve data integration capabilities.

options.label: No references to other data
options.feedback: Consider adding references to related datasets to improve discoverability and context.

categories.title: Reusable
categories.description: The ultimate goal of FAIR is to optimize the reuse of data. To achieve this, metadata and data should be well-described so they can be replicated and/or combined in different settings.

questions.title: Rich Attributes
questions.question: Are your data and metadata richly described with accurate and relevant attributes?
questions.explanation: Rich descriptions help users understand the data's context, limitations, and appropriate use cases for reuse.

options.label: Comprehensive documentation with methodology, quality, and context
options.feedback: Excellent! Comprehensive documentation enables confident reuse.

options.label: Good documentation covering most important aspects
options.feedback: Good coverage! Consider adding more detail about methodology and limitations.

options.label: Basic documentation with some details
options.feedback: Basic documentation is present but could be enriched for better reuse.

options.label: Minimal documentation
options.feedback: Enhance documentation with methodology, quality information, and usage context.

questions.title: Clear Usage License
questions.question: Are your data and metadata released with a clear and accessible data usage license?
questions.explanation: Clear licensing removes legal barriers to reuse and helps users understand how they can use your data.

options.label: CC0 (Public Domain)
options.feedback: Excellent! CC0 provides maximum reuse freedom.

options.label: CC BY (Attribution required)
options.feedback: Great! CC BY enables broad reuse with proper attribution.

options.label: Other open license
options.feedback: Good! Open licenses facilitate reuse. Consider CC licenses for broader recognition.

options.label: Restrictive license
options.feedback: Restrictive licenses limit reuse. Consider more open alternatives if possible.

options.label: No clear license
options.feedback: Add a clear license to enable legal reuse of your data.

questions.title: Provenance
questions.question: Are your data and metadata associated with detailed provenance information?
questions.explanation: Provenance information helps users understand the data's origin, processing history, and reliability for their use cases.

options.label: Detailed provenance with full processing history
options.feedback: Excellent! Detailed provenance enables confident reuse and reproducibility.

options.label: Good provenance information covering key aspects
options.feedback: Good coverage! Consider adding more detail about processing steps and tools used.

options.label: Basic provenance information
options.feedback: Basic provenance is present but could be enhanced with more processing details.

options.label: Minimal or no provenance information
options.feedback: Add provenance information including data sources, processing steps, and tools used.

questions.title: Community Standards
questions.question: Do your data and metadata meet domain-relevant community standards?
questions.explanation: Following community standards ensures that your data fits well with existing practices and tools in your field.

options.label: Fully compliant with community standards
options.feedback: Excellent! Community standards compliance ensures seamless integration.

options.label: Mostly compliant with minor gaps
options.feedback: Good compliance! Work on closing the remaining gaps.

options.label: Partially compliant with community standards
options.feedback: Work towards better compliance with community standards for improved reuse.

options.label: Minimal compliance with standards
options.feedback: Identify and implement relevant community standards for your domain.

options.label: No awareness of community standards
options.feedback: Research and adopt relevant community standards for your field.

levels.description: Your dataset has significant gaps in FAIR compliance. Focus on implementing basic FAIR principles.
recommendations: Start with unique identifiers for your datasets

levels.description: Your dataset has basic FAIR compliance but could benefit from enhancements.
recommendations: Enhance metadata with more detailed descriptions

levels.description: Your dataset demonstrates good FAIR compliance with room for optimization.
recommendations: Adopt semantic web formats for better interoperability
Add detailed provenance and methodology information
Register in multiple repositories for better discoverability

levels.description: Your dataset demonstrates excellent FAIR compliance and serves as a model for others.
recommendations: Share your practices with the community
Consider becoming a FAIR champion in your field
Continuously update practices as standards evolve
